pattern: The fewest perfect squares summing to n is an unbounded coin-change over square denominations, solved by a bottom-up table where each value takes one plus the best of subtracting any square.
hints: This is a shortest-combination problem: how few square numbers add up to n, where each square may be used repeatedly.
Define dp[i] as the minimum count for i; it depends on dp[i minus a square] for every square not exceeding i.
Initialize dp[0]=0, and for each i set dp[i] to one plus the smallest dp[i minus j*j] over all j with j*j <= i; return dp[n].

edge_inputs.description: smallest input, one is a perfect square
a number of the 4^a(8b+7) form requires four squares

stress.description: random n across the full range so all of the one, two, three and four square cases appear
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    return [rng.randrange(1, max(2, min(size, 10000)) + 1)]
